coredata.dc:title: A highly sensitive and accurate SERS/RRS dual-spectroscopic immunosensor for clenbuterol based on nitrogen/silver-codoped carbon dots catalytic amplification
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Dongmei
Li, Chongning
Wen, Guiqing
Liang, Aihui
Jiang, Zhiliang
coredata.dc:description: Preparation of multifunctional codoped carbon dots, and their new analytical applications in surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) quantitative analytical method are still challenge. To overcome these problems, the nitrogen/silver-codoped carbon dots (CDN/Ag) with highly catalytic amplification are prepared by microwave method, and characterized by spectrophotometry and electron microscopy. The results show that CDN/Ag can strongly catalyze trisodium citrate-HAuCl4 reaction to generate red nanogold with resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) effect and SERS effect using Victoria blue B (VBB) as molecular probes. The CDN/Ag catalytic amplification and specific immunoreaction of clenbuterol (Clen) are coupled with highly sensitive SERS and accurate RRS to fabricate a new dual-spectroscopic strategy with a detection limit of 0.68 pg mL−1 Clen.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SERS/RRS dual-spectroscopy
N/Ag-doped carbon dots
Catalysis
Immunosensor
Clenbuterol

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (CDN)
Synthesis of nitrogen and silver co-doped carbon dots by hydrothermal method (CDN/Ag(HT))
Synthesis of nitrogen and silver co-doped carbon dots by microwave method (CDN/Ag)
Synthesis of silver doped carbon dots (CDAg)
Electron microscope and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS)
Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS)
Nanoparticle size distribution and nanoparticle zeta potential analysis of the analytical systems
Catalysis of the carbon dots and inhibition of the antibody

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The formation of CDN/Ag and its principle for catalytic detection of Clen.

A: SERS spectra of CDN/Ag4–HAuCl4-TC system. a: 0.29 mM HAuCl4 +0.87 mM HCl +0.32 mM TC+0.87 μM VBB; b: a+0.03 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4; c: a+0.06 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4; d: a+0.13 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4; e: a+0.19 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4; f: a+0.32 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4; g: a+0.52 μg mL−1 CDNAg4; h: a+0.64 μg mL−1 CDNAg4. B: SERS spectra of Ab-CDN/Ag4–HAuCl4-TC system. a: 0.64 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4 +0.29 mM HAuCl4 +0.87 mM HCl +0.32 mM TC+0.87 μM VBB; b: a+0.01 ng mL−1 Ab; c: a+0.02 ng mL−1 Ab; d: a+0.04 ng mL−1 Ab; e: a+0.06 ng mL−1 Ab; f: a+0.10 ng mL−1 Ab; g: a+0.16 ng mL−1 Ab. C: SERS spectra of Clen-Ab-CDN/Ag4– HAuCl4-TC system. a: 0.64 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4 +0.16 ng mL−1 Ab +0.29 mM HAuCl4 +0.87 mM HCl +0.32 mM TC+0.87 μM VBB; b: a+0.0033 ng mL−1 Clen; c: a+0.0067 ng mL−1 Clen; d: a+0.013 ng mL−1 Clen; e: a+0.020 ng mL−1 Clen; f: a+0.033 ng mL−1 Clen; g: a+0.053 ng mL−1 Clen; h: a+0.067 ng mL−1 Clen.

A: RRS spectra of CDN/Ag4–HAuCl4-TC system. a: 0.29 mM HAuCl4 +0.87 mM HCl +0.32 mM TC; b: a+0.06 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4; c: a+0.13 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4; d: a+0.19 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4; e: a+0.32 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4; f: a+0.52 μg mL−1 CDNAg4; g: a+0.64 μg mL−1 CDNAg4; h: a+0.84 μg mL−1 CDNAg4. B: RRS spectra of Ab-CDN/Ag4–HAuCl4-TC system. a: 0.64 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4 +0.29 mM HAuCl4 +0.87 mM HCl +0.32 mM TC; b: a+0.015 ng mL−1 Ab; c: a+0.02 ng mL−1 Ab; d: a+0.04 ng mL−1 Ab; e: a+0.06 ng mL−1 Ab; f: a+0.10 ng mL−1 Ab; g: a+0.16 ng mL−1 Ab. C: RRS spectra of Clen-Ab- CDN/Ag4–HAuCl4-TC system. a: 0.64 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4 +0.16 ng mL−1 Ab +0.29 mM HAuCl4 +0.87 mM HCl +0.32 mM TC; b: a+0.0067 ng mL−1 Clen; c: a+0.013 ng mL−1 Clen; d: a+0.020 ng mL−1 Clen; e: a+0.033 ng mL−1 Clen; f: a+0.053 ng mL−1 Clen; g: a+0.067 ng mL−1 Clen.

A: UV–visible absorption spectra of Au/AgNP and carbon dots (120 mg L−1 AuNP; 32.4 mg L−1 AgNP; 0.58 mg mL−1 CDN; 8.6 mg mL−1 CDAg; 0.58 mg mL−1 CDN/Ag(HT); 0.58 mg mL−1 CDN/Ag); B: UV–visible absorption spectra of Clen-Ab- CDN/Ag4– HAuCl4- TC system. a: 0.64 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4 +0.16 ng mL−1 Ab +0.29 mM HAuCl4 +0.87 mM HCl +0.32 mM TC; b: a+0.013 ng mL−1 Clen; c: a+0.020 ng mL−1 Clen; d: a+0.033 ng mL−1 Clen; e: a+0.053 ng mL−1 Clen; f: a+0.067 ng mL−1 Clen.

A: TEM and EDS of CDN/Ag; b: SEM and EDS of AuNP (0.64 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4 +0.16 ng mL−1 Ab +0.29 mM HAuCl4 +0.87 mM HCl +0.32 mM TC); c: SEM and EDS of AuNP (0.64 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag4 +0.16 ng mL−1 Ab +0.053 ng mL−1 Clen +0.29 mM HAuCl4 +0.87 mM HCl +0.32 mM TC).
A: FTIR of carbon dots; B: XPS of carbon dots.
Analysis characteristics for the SERS/RRS dual-spectroscopic immunoassay of Clen.

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive and accurate SERS/RRS dual-spectroscopic immunosensor for clenbuterol based on nitrogen/silver-codoped carbon dots catalytic amplification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University), Ministry of Education, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin, 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University)
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University), Ministry of Education, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Biology Engineering, Hechi University, Yizhou, 546300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Biology Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Biology Engineering, Hechi University, Yizhou 546300, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparation of multifunctional codoped carbon dots, and their new analytical applications in surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) quantitative analytical method are still challenge. To overcome these problems, the nitrogen/silver-codoped carbon dots (CDN/Ag) with highly catalytic amplification are prepared by microwave method, and characterized by spectrophotometry and electron microscopy. The results show that CDN/Ag can strongly catalyze trisodium citrate-HAuCl4 reaction to generate red nanogold with resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) effect and SERS effect using Victoria blue B (VBB) as molecular probes. The CDN/Ag catalytic amplification and specific immunoreaction of clenbuterol (Clen) are coupled with highly sensitive SERS and accurate RRS to fabricate a new dual-spectroscopic strategy with a detection limit of 0.68 pg mL−1 Clen.

Schematic diagram of detecting Clen by SERS/RRS dual-spectroscopy.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CD) is a carbon-based zero-dimensional material within 10 nm of the particle size. They have excellent optical properties, thermal and chemical stability, good electron conductivity and biocompatibility. Now, they have been widely used in food safety, biomedical and environmental analysis [1,3–11]. Xu et al. prepared a sphere CD with a particle size of 5 nm and a quantum yield of 10.37%. Based on the fluorescence quenching effect of lemon yellow on CD, the detection of lemon yellow in food was realized [1]. However, low quantum yield and lack of functionalization make the carbon dots limited in bioconjugation and further applications. Studies have shown that surface modification and doping of electron donors are effective ways to improve the CD chemical properties and broaden their application range [2]. So far, the application of single-element doped carbon dots in quantitative analysis has been reported. A P-doped carbon dot was prepared for the determination of 1 nM Cu2+ in water [6]. The S-doped carbon dot with a 3.2 nm particle size was prepared for the detection of 0.1–1.0 mM Fe3+ [7]. Although the single-element doped CDs have huge advantages, they also have defects in the limited spectral range of the luminescence. Studies have shown that this defect can be solved by simultaneously doping more than two elements, which is called “co-doping” [8]. At present, the most important synthesis method for carbon dots is hydrothermal method, and less microwave methods reported. The application of carbon dots in analytical chemistry is mostly based on fluorescence method, and the synthesis of CDN/Ag by microwave method and its application in surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS)/RRS dual-spectroscopic catalytic analysis have not been found.

SERS is a very important molecular spectral technique. Due to its high sensitivity, SERS has attracted more and more attention in the field of analysis. SERS technology for immunoassay has also been applied in several fields, such as food safety, biomedicine and environmental monitoring [12,13]. Although SERS is sensitive, its reproducibility needs to be further improved. At the same time, the resonance Rayleigh scattering spectroscopy (RRS) has been used in the determination of small molecules, biomacromolecules, and nanoparticle research, with simple operation, low cost and good reproducibility [14,15]. At present, the application of SERS or RRS in the field of quantitative analysis has been studied, but there are few reports on the coupling of SERS and RRS to establish a SERS/RRS dual mode method for Clenbuterol (Clen) coupled with immuoreaction, in addition SERS/fluorescence [16–20], fluorescence/chirality [21] and SERS/magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [22]. This SERS/RRS dual-spectroscopic approach combines the sensitive SERS with the sensitive and reproducible RRS for better performance.

Clen is a variety of “lean meat powder”, which promotes protein deposition and lipolysis in animals, and large doses used in feed can promote pig growth, reduce fat content, and increase lean meat rate, but eating pork containing Clen is harmful to humans. Therefore, Clen has been banned in most countries. However, some illegal traders are still using this additive to harm people's health for the benefit. Therefore, the quantitative analysis of clenbuterol in food is particularly meaningful. Currently, the detection methods for Clen are SERS [13], chromatography [23–25], enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [26–28], electrochemical method [29–32], fluorescence method [33–35], mass spectrometry [36], spectrophotometry [37,38] and so on. A SERS method was developed to detect 17 fg mL−1 Clen using immunomagnetic nanogold as substrate [22]. The method needs to be separated and complicated. An immunochromatographic analysis technology was developed to immediate detect Clen using SiO2 nanoparticles as the optical direct detection tag [23]. The method is complicated and time consuming. The ELISA method requires the preparation of a special kit that is expensive [27]. A double-label fluorescence probe consisting of Eu3+-label IgG and Sm3+-label IgG1 was used to detect 0.007 ng mL−1 Clen [35]. This method requires fluorescent labeling and may increase background interference. To our best knowledge, the rapid microwave preparation of CDN/Ag and its application in catalytic immunoassay have not been found. In this paper, high catalytic activity carbon dots doped with different elements were prepared and their catalytic activities were also compared. Based on the inhibition of CDN/Ag catalytic activity by Clen antibody and the specific immune response between Clen antibody and antigen, a simple and sensitive immunological regulation of CDN/Ag activity SERS/RRS dual-spectroscopic assay was established to detect Clen.

The Raman spectrometer was purchased from Thermo Company (DXR, USA), with a 633 nm laser wavelength, a 3.0 mW laser power, a 50 μm slit, and a 5 s acquisition time. The fluorescence spectrophotometer was purchased from Varian Company (Cary Eclipse, USA). UV–visible spectrophotometer was purchased from Beijing General Instrument Co., Ltd. (TU-1901, China). Transmission electron microscope was purchased from JEOL Ltd. (JEM 2100F, Japan). Scanning electron microscope was purchased from Hi-Tech Corporation/Oxford Company (S-4800, Japan/UK). Multi-flux closed microwave chemical workstation was purchased from Shanghai Xinyi Microwave Chemical Technology Company (JUPITER-B, China). The nanoparticle size and Zeta potential analyzer was purchased from Malvern Company (England). The constant temperature water bath kettle was purchased from Jintan Dadi Automatic Instrument Company (HH–S2, China).

Clenbuterol antibody (3 mg mL−1, Wuhan Huamei Bioengineering Limited Company, Wuhan, China), Clenbuterol-BSA conjugate (1 mg mL−1, Wuhan Huamei Bioengineering Limited Company, Wuhan, China) (Added 1 mL of water to 1 mg of Clen lyophilized powder, after thoroughly mixing, a Clen stock solution was obtained), 6.8 mM fructose (Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Limited Company, Shanghai, China), AgNO3 (Xilong Scientific Limited Company, Guangzhou, China), NaBH4 (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Limited Company, Shanghai, China), trisodium citrate (TC) (Shanghai Reagent Factory, Shanghai, China), 2.9 mM HAuCl4 (Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute, Tianjin, China), 0.01 M HCl (Xilong Scientific Limited Company, Guangzhou, China), 10 μM VBB (China Pharmaceutical Shanghai Chemical Reagent Station, Shanghai, China), and urea (Xiqiao Chemical Limited Company, Guangzhou, China) were used. All reagents are of analytical grade and the water used is double distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (CDN)
ce:section.ce:para: In a 100 mL beaker, 1 g fructose, 1 g urea and 30 mL water were added, after ultrasonic dissolution, the solution was transferred to an autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 5 h. After the product was naturally cooled, it was centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm/g, and the supernatant was dialyzed for 12 h in a dialysis bag with a 3500 Da molecular interception. The carbon dots solution was vacuum freeze-dried to obtain a product with a weight of 0.8598 g, and the concentration was calculated to be 0.029 g mL−1 CDN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen and silver co-doped carbon dots by hydrothermal method (CDN/Ag(HT))
ce:section.ce:para: CDN/Ag(HT) is prepared in the same way as CDN, and the detailed reagents are as follows: 1 g of fructose, 1.0 g of urea and 3 mL 0.01 M AgNO3. The carbon dots solution was vacuum freeze-dried to obtain a product with a weight of 0.8781 g, and the concentration was calculated to be 0.029 g mL−1 CDN/Ag(HT).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen and silver co-doped carbon dots by microwave method (CDN/Ag)
ce:section.ce:para: In a 100 mL beaker, 1 g fructose, 1 g urea, a certain amount of 10 mM AgNO3 (0.5 mL, 1 mL, 2 mL, 3 mL and 5 mL) and 30 mL water were added, after ultrasonic dissolution, the solution was transferred to microwave digestion tank and reacted at 400 W and 200 °C for 40 min. After naturally cooled, the product was dialyzed for 12 h in a dialysis bag with a 3500 Da molecular interception. The carbon dots solution was vacuum freeze-dried to obtain a product with a weight of 0.8592 g, and the concentration was calculated to be 0.029 g mL−1 CDN/Ag.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of silver doped carbon dots (CDAg)
ce:section.ce:para: In a 50 mL Erlenmeyer flask, 3 mL of 0.025 g mL−1 CD0 (prepared by hydrothermal method with 1.0 g fructose) and 3 mL 10 mM AgNO3 and 4 mL water were added, and the solution was heated in a 100 °C water bath for 20 min. After naturally cooled, the carbon dots solution was vacuum freeze-dried to obtain a product with a weight of 0.0864 g, and the concentration was calculated to be 8.6 mg mL−1 CDAg.

In a clean 50 mL Erlenmeyer flask, 40 mL water, 3.5 mL 0.034 M TC and 925 μL 10 mM AgNO3 were added in turn, and the solution was placed on a 30 °C thermostat magnetic stirrer. And then, 4 mL 100 mM NaBH4 was added dropwise until the solution became a yellow transparent solution, and stirring was continued for 15 min to obtain a silver nanosol with a concentration of 32.4 mg L−1 AgNP (calculated as AgNO3).

In a clean conical flask, 42.5 mL water, 3.5 mL 0.034 M TC and 500 μL 2.9 × 10−2 M HAuCl4 were added in turn under stirring. And then 3.5 mL 0.5 mg mL−1 NaBH4 was added dropwise, finally the solution color changes to dark red. The solution was continuously stirred for 10 min to obtain a concentration of 120 mg L−1 AuNP (calculated as HAuCl4).

30 μL 3.0 ng mL−1 clenbuterol antibody (Ab) and 100 μL 9.7 μg mL−1 CDN/Ag were added into the graduated tube, after standing for 10 min, a certain concentration of Clen was added, mixed well and stand for 15 min. Then 150 μL 2.9 mM HAuCl4, 130 μL 0.01 M HCl and 70 μL of 6.8 mM TC were added in turn, mix well and dilute to a volume of 1.5 mL. After responding 20 min in the 50 °C water bath, the reaction was stopped with ice water and 0.13 mL 0.01 mM VBB was added to the solution. The Raman spectrum was obtained by Raman spectrometer. The SERS intensity (I) at 1615 cm−1 was measured, while the SERS intensity of blank (I0) was measured without adding Clen, and ΔI = I - I0 was calculated. The corresponding RRS intensity was obtained simply without addition of the molecular probes.

The doping of chemical heteroatoms has been favored by researchers as an important way to improve the performance of carbon dots. Here, based on the microwave irradiation technique, nitrogen and silver single-element doping and two-elements codoping carbon dots are prepared. Under the conditions, fructose and urea undergo dehydration condensation reaction to form carbon dots with amino group on the surface. Through the specific binding of –NH2 and –OH to Ag, the highly catalytically active carbon dots doped with N and Ag are finally obtained (Fig. 1 ). Both N and Ag are effective carbon dot dopants, mainly because the five valence electrons of the N element can provide excess electrons for the carbon dots, and the atomic size is comparable to C, which leads to changes in optical properties [39]. Noble metal nanoparticles act as excellent electron donors, their loading on the carbon dots is also a method to improve the catalytic performance of the carbon dots. It can extend the visible light absorption capability by a strong electromagnetic field formed by local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) to produces more photogenerated electrons and holes. Among the precious metals, Ag is the cheapest metal with the strongest LSPR effect. Among the six nanoparticles, CDN/Ag has the strongest catalytic activity, which may be attributed to the synergistic effect of Ag and N on the catalytic performance of the system is very good.

The reduction of HAuCl4 by TC is slow without nanocatalyst. When CDN/Ag is added, the CDN/Ag can rapidly catalyze the reaction to form red nanogold that exhibits a strong RRS signal. After adding VBB, the system also exhibits a strong Raman signal. Based on the electrostatic coupling between CDN/Ag and Ab, the catalytic activity of CDN/Ag was inhibited and the Raman signal was reduced. Upon addition of Clen, based on the specific immunoreaction between Ab and Clen, Clen can desorb the Ab on the CDN/Ag surface and the catalytic activity of CDN/Ag was restored. As the concentration of Clen increases, the catalytically active CDN/Ag increases, and the generated gold nanoparticles increase, and the Raman signal enhances (Fig. 1). More simply, the RRS intensity at 607 nm enhanced with Clen concentration increasing without addition of molecular probes of VBB. According to the above facts, a new SERS/RRS dual-spectroscopic method for immunomodulating CDN/Ag activity was established to detect Clen.

Under the condition of 50 °C water bath, the HAuCl4-trisodium citrate system is difficult to react. When CDN/Ag is present, HAuCl4-trisodium citrate system can be quickly catalyzed by CDN/Ag to from red nanogold. After adding VBB, the system has strong Raman peaks at 1615, 1394 and 1200 cm−1. The peaks at 1200 cm−1, 1394 cm−1 and 1615 cm−1 belong to –NH2 torsional deformation, CC and C–H bending vibrations, and benzene ring-induced stretching vibrations of CC and CN respectively. Among them, the Raman peak at 1615 cm−1 is the strongest (Fig. 2 A), the Raman intensity is linearly increasing with the catalyst amount of CDN/Ag.

The catalytic activity of C–N–Ag carbon dots on HAuCl4-TC reaction was investigated by using fructose as C precursor, AgNO3 as Ag source and urea as N source. Based on the doping of N element, different amounts of Ag doping have different catalytic effects on HAuCl4-TC system, and CDN/Ag4 has the strongest catalytic effect on the system (Table 1 ). Therefore, CDN/Ag4 was selected as a catalyst for the HAuCl4-TC system. When Ab was added, carbon dots can be coated by Ab and the catalytic activity of CDN/Ag4 of the system was inhibited. As the Ab concentration increases, the system SERS signal weakens gradually (Fig. 2B). With the addition of Clen, Clen and Ab reacted specifically to detach the Ab from the surface of CDN/Ag4, the catalytic activity of CDN/Ag4 was restored, and the amount of nanogold formed increased. With the Clen concentration increase, the SERS signal increase linearly with the Clen concentration (Fig. 2C).

Similarly, the SERS spectra of CDN system (Fig. S1), CDN/Ag(HT) system (Fig. S2), AgNP system (Fig. S3), AuNP system (Fig. S4) and CDAg system (Fig. S5) for the detection of Clen were investigated. The results show that the sensitivity of CDN/Ag system is the highest, followed by CDN, AgNP and CDAg, and the sensitivity of AuNP is the weakest.

RRS is a sensitive and reproducible quantitative analytical molecular spectroscopy technique. RRS is a simple and sensitive molecular spectral technique, and was good tool to investigate nanoreaction. Thus, it was chosen for study the naogold reaction, in addition for correction and comparison of the SERS method. The HAuCl4-TC system reacted slowly at 50 °C water bath. When CDN/Ag4 is present, it can quickly catalyze the redox reaction of HAuCl4-TC to generate red gold nanoparticles, and exhibits a RRS peak at 607 nm (Fig. 3 A). As the concentration of CDN/Ag4 increased, the amount of nanogold generated increased, and the RRS signal at 607 nm increased gradually. After the addition of Ab, the catalytic ability of CDN/Ag4 can be inhibited by Ab due to the adsorption of Ab to CDN/Ag4, and the RRS signal decreases gradually as the concentration of the Ab increases (Fig. 3B). When Clen is added, based on the specific reaction between Clen and Ab, Ab can desorb from the surface of CDN/Ag4 to restore the catalysis of the system. As the concentration of Clen increased, the amount of nanogold produced by the system increased and the RRS signal increased gradually (Fig. 3C). Similarly, as the concentration of Clen increased, the amount of nanogold generated by CDN, CDN/Ag(HT), AgNP, AuNP and CDAg systems increased, and the RRS signal increased gradually (Figs. S6–10). Among them, the CDN/Ag system was the most sensitive, and the CDN/Ag sol was very stable.

After the carbon dots were prepared according to the experimental method, their excitation spectra and fluorescence spectra were examined under the conditions of a voltage of 500 V and a slit of 10 nm (Fig. S11A). It can be seen from Fig. S11A that when the excitation wavelength is 275 nm, CDN/Ag(HT) exhibits a fluorescence peak at 450 nm. Meanwhile, the excitation wavelength and fluorescence wavelength of CDN are consistent with CDN/Ag(HT), which indicates that the particle size of these two carbon dots may be similar. Compared with CDN/Ag(HT), the fluorescence peak of CDN/Ag has a slight blue shift. At the excitation wavelength of 275 nm, the fluorescence peak of CDN/Ag appears at about 430 nm, which may be related to the different preparation methods. From the fluorescence intensities of CDN/Ag, CDN/Ag(HT) and CDN, it can be seen that at the same concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots is increased after doping with Ag element. Under the excitation wavelength of 246 nm, CDAg shows a fluorescence peak around 344 nm, but the intensity is very weak, which indicates that the carbon dot prepared by the water bath method is not effective. Based on the fluorescence of CDN/Ag, the possibility of using CDN/Ag as a fluorescent source to establish a fluorescence method for detecting Clen was investigated. However, under the experimental conditions, no obvious fluorescence peak and law of change in fluorescence intensity were observed in the Clen-Ab-CDN/Ag4–HAuCl4-TC system (Fig. S11B), and a strong RRS appeared at 280 nm. This is mainly because the CDN/Ag4 fluorescence intensity is weak at this concentration and the Rayleigh scattering is strong, which indicate that the catalytic RRS/SERS method is more sensitive than the fluorescence method.

The UV–visible absorption spectra of different nanomaterials were investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 4 A, AuNP and AgNP have absorption peaks at 517 nm and 394 nm, respectively, which are the surface plasmon absorption peaks of Au and Ag, respectively. The absorption peak of CDN is around 355 nm. After doping with Ag element, the absorption peaks of CDN/Ag and CDN/Ag(HT) appear around 350 nm, and they have a slight blue shift. This phenomenon may be related to their structure. CDAg has an absorption peak at 321 nm, which is significantly blue-shifted compared to other carbon dots. It can be seen from the absorption peaks of these carbon dots that their absorption peaks are single peaks, which means that they are present in the form of a complex after doping with N and Ag, rather than being present in separate substances.

As can be seen from Fig. 4B, the CDN/Ag4 system has an absorption peak around 550 nm, and this absorption peak belongs to the nanogold. With the concentration of Clen increases, the desorbed CDN/Ag4 increases, the amount of nanogold formed by the system increases, and the absorption peak signal at 550 nm increases gradually. Similarly, CDN, CDN/Ag(HT), AgNP, AuNP and CDAg systems all have an absorption peak around 550 nm. With the increases of Clen concentration, the system generated nanogold increases, the absorption peak signal at 550 nm enhances gradually (Figs. S12–16). Among them, the CDN/Ag system is the most sensitive.

The highly sensitive and simple RRS technique was selected to study the sol stability. The nanosol was allowed to place at normal pressure and temperature for half a month, and the RRS values (Fig. S17) of the carbon dots at different times were measured. The results show that AgNP and AuNP remain stable for 6 days and 12 days, respectively. After 6 days and 12 days, the absorbance value decrease and the resonance Rayleigh scattering value increase, respectively. CDN and CDN/Ag remain stable in 8 days, after 8 days, the absorbance value and RRS value decrease. The absorbance value of CDAg gradually increases in the first 5 days, and then tends to be stable, at the same time, its RRS value first decreases, then tends to stabilize, and finally decreases. The stability of nanosols in NaCl solution was studied. The results show that CDN and CDN/Ag could be stably existed in NaCl solution. When the NaCl solution reached a certain concentration, AgNP and AuNP will aggregate, and the color of the solution changed, and the RRS value first increased and then decreased, and finally stabilized (Fig. S18). Overall, CDN and CDN/Ag are more stable than CDAg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron microscope and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS)
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots were prepared according to the experimental method. After diluting a certain number of times, 20 μL of the solution was dropped onto a copper mesh and scanned by a transmission electron microscope (TEM). As can be seen from Fig. 5 a, CDN/Ag tends to have a spherical structure with a 7 nm average particle dimension. The reaction solution was obtained by the experimental method, and the supernatant was moved by centrifugation for 10 min at 7000 rpm/g, and the resulting precipitate was diluted to 1.5 mL. Then 5 μL of the above solution was added to the silicon wafer, after drying, the morphology of the system was examined by scanning electron microscope (SEM). It can be seen that in the absence of Clen, the system produced less nanogold, and its average particle size is 60 nm (Fig. 5b). As the concentration of Clen increased, the amount of nanogold increased gradually (Fig. 5c). The sample solution was prepared according to the experimental method, and after diluting a certain number of times, 5 μL of the solution was added to the silicon wafer, after drying, and the energy spectrum (EDS) was recorded by SEM. As can be seen from Fig. 5a, CDN/Ag mainly contains four elements of C, O, N and Ag (Fig. 5a). The results showed that in the absence of Clen, the system formed less nanogold, and the content of Au element in EDS is less (Fig. 5b). As the concentration of Clen increased, the amount of nanogold formed by the system increased gradually, and the content of Au element in EDS increased gradually (Fig. 5c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS)
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots were prepared according to the experimental method, and after the samples were vacuum-dried, they were mixed with KBr to be ground and compressed, and scanned by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer to obtain the infrared spectra (Fig. 6 A). It can be seen from the FTIR of CDN/Ag that CDN/Ag has a broad peak at 3383-3200 cm−1, which may be caused by stretching vibration of O–H and N–H. The absorption peak at 1678 cm−1 may be caused by the stretching vibration of CO. The absorption peaks at 1631 and 1595 cm−1 may be related to the skeletal vibration of the benzene ring. The absorption peak at 1437 and 1401 cm−1 may be the in-plane bending vibration of C–H. The absorption peaks at 1347, 1298, 1113 and 1089 cm−1 may be caused by the stretching vibration of amide and C–N. Based on the analysis of FTIR, we speculate that the structure of CDN/Ag is shown in Fig. 1. From the FTIR of CDN/Ag(HT) and CDAg, it can be seen that they have little difference from the FTIR of CDN/Ag, and their structure should be similar. The identification of Ag elements requires the assistance of EDS (Fig. 5a). The FTIR of CDAg is significantly different from CDN/Ag at 1390-1145 cm−1, mainly because of its lack of N element.

At the same time, the XPS of the carbon dots was investigated. In Fig. 6B, the XPS spectrum of CDAg reveals characteristic peaks at 532 eV for O 1s, 285 eV for C 1s, 97 eV for Ag 4s; the XPS spectrum of CDN reveals characteristic peaks at 285 eV for C 1s, 400 eV for N 1s, 532 eV for O 1s; the XPS spectrum of CDN/Ag(HT) reveals characteristic peaks at 285 eV for C 1s, 400 eV for N 1s, 532 eV for O 1s, 97 eV for Ag 4s; the XPS spectrum of CDN/Ag reveals characteristic peaks at 285 eV for C 1s, 400 eV for N 1s, 532 eV for O 1s, 97 eV for Ag 4s. The XPS results show that the carbon dots are successfully doped with N, Ag or N/Ag elements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanoparticle size distribution and nanoparticle zeta potential analysis of the analytical systems
ce:section.ce:para: The sample solution was prepared according to the experimental method and its nanoparticle size was recorded using a nanoparticle size and Zeta potential analyzer. It can be seen from Fig. S19 that the average particle size of CDN/Ag is 7.5 nm that is in agreement with the result of transmission electron microscope. In the absence of Clen, the system produced less nanogold and has a small nanopartical size (d = 61 nm). With the increased of Clen concentration, the amount of nanogold generated by the system increased gradually, the nanoparticle size of the system increased gradually (Fig. S19).

The sample solution was prepared according to the experimental method and its Zeta potential was recorded by a nanoparticle size and Zeta potential analyzer. The results show that CDN and CDN/Ag both are negatively charged, and the Zeta potential of stable CDN/Ag (−31.3 mV) is larger than CDN (−20.4 mV) (Figs. S20a and b). This result is agreement with the catalytic result of CDs, which indicates that the larger the zeta potential, the stronger the catalytic ability of the carbon dots. In the absence of Clen, the system generated less nanogold and the Zeta potential (−36.5 mV) is larger (Fig. S20c). With the increase of Clen concentration, the amount of nanogold formed by the system increase gradually, and the Zeta potential (−33.4 mV, −27.1 mV) decrease gradually (Fig. S20 d, e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalysis of the carbon dots and inhibition of the antibody
ce:section.ce:para: Under the experimental conditions, CDN, CDN/Ag, CDAg, AuNP and AgNP can catalyze the redox of HAuCl4-trisodium citrate to generate nanogold particles. In a certain concentration range, with the catalyst concentration increases, their catalytic ability enhances, and the generated nanogold increases. The SERS signal at 1615 cm−1 increase linearly with the catalyst concentration. Among them, when N and Ag are doped at the same time, CDN/Ag4 has the strongest catalytic effect on the system. Among the six types of nanomaterials, CDN/Ag has the strongest catalytic activity, followed by AgNP, AuNP, CDN/Ag(HT) and CDAg, and the catalytic effect of CDN is the weakest (Table 1). When the Ab was added to the system, carbon dots can adsorb on Ab surface via electrostatic interaction, and its catalytic activity is inhibited. As the amount of Ab increases, the catalytic activity of the system decreases, and the SERS effect weakened. The weakened SERS intensity has a linearly relationship with the Ab concentration. Among them, CDN/Ag4 system has the strongest inhibitory effect, followed by CDN/Ag(HT), CDN, CDAg and AgNP, and the inhibition of AuNP system is the weakest (Table 2 ). At the same time, by comparing the catalytic performance of CDN/Ag prepared by hydrothermal method and microwave method, it can be seen that CDN/Ag prepared by microwave method has better catalytic performance and shorter preparation time.

Under the experimental conditions, the catalytic activity of the N-doped carbon dot is weak, and the catalytic activity increases with the increase of the doping amount of Ag (Table 1). The carbon dot is an excellent electron acceptor, and the electrons of Ag can be transferred to the surface of the carbon dot, so that the electron density on the surface of the carbon dot increases. In the absence of a catalyst, trisodium citrate is difficult to reduce HAuCl4 to gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) due to low electrons. With the addition of CDN/Ag, based on the surface electrons of CDN/Ag can be transferred to the Au3+ surface rapidly, which allows Au3+ to acquire electrons to generate Au. Au as a seed can further catalyze the HAuCl4-TC reaction to grow into AuNPs (Fig. 7 ).

The influence of reagent concentration on the SERS determination of Clen is studied respectively. The reagent concentration is chosen for use when the ΔI is the maximum. Results show that when the CDN/Ag concentration is 0.56 μg mL−1 (Fig. S21), the Ab concentration is 0.16 ng mL−1 (Fig. S22), the HCl concentration is 0.87 mM (Fig. S23), the HAuCl4 concentration is 0.29 mM (Fig. S24), the TC concentration is 0.32 mM (Fig. S25), the VBB concentration is 0.83 μM (Fig. S26), the rest time (1) is 10 min (Fig. S27), the rest time (2) is 15 min (Fig. S28), the water bath temperature is 50 °C (Fig. S29), the water bath time is 21 min (Fig. S30), the ΔI value is at its maximum and these parameters are therefore considered to be the optimum conditions.

Under the optimal experimental condition, the linear relationship was drawn with different concentrations of Clen and corresponding ΔI. The linear relationships of nanosols and their corresponding features are listed in Table 3 . Among the SERS and RRS linear relationships, the slope of the regression equation of CDN/Ag system is the largest and its sensitivity is the highest. At the same time, the linear slope of the SERS working curve of all nanosols is larger than that of RRS, indicating that SERS is more sensitive. However, by comparing the precision of CDN/Ag catalytic system (Fig. S31), the precision of RRS is better. Through the correction of RRS, the sensitivity of SERS is guaranteed while its accuracy is guaranteed. Currently, there are few preparation methods for CDN/Ag. Compared with the reported preparation methods of CDN/Ag (Table S1) [40–42], this method is simple, short in times, and the prepared CDN/Ag are more stable and uniform. At the same time, compare with other methods to detect Clen (Table S2), the SERS/RRS dual-spectroscopic method is sensitive and reproducible, that is a facile method to detect Clen.

Based on the experimental method, the effect of interfering substances on Clen detection is investigated. The results show that relative error is within ±10%, common amino acids and ions have no effect on the measurement of 0.033 ng mL−1 Clen (Table S3), indicating that the method has a good selectivity.

Three kinds of pork liver samples were taken from the market. After mincing, 2 g samples were weighed, and then 4 mL of 0.1 M HCl was added, sonicated for 15 min, after centrifugation for 5 min at 12,100 rpm/g, the supernatant was collected. The precipitate was extracted twice with 0.1 M HCl 4 mL and 2 mL, respectively, and the supernatant was merged to obtain 10 mL of pork liver sample solution. The Clen content was determined according to the procedure, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) between 4.57 and 5.76% and a recovery between 92.1 and 93.4% (Table S4).

In this paper, the CDN/Ag is prepared by microwave method. Compared with other nanosols, the CDN/Ag has the strongest catalytic ability for the reaction of trisodium citrate reduce HAuCl4 to form nanogold, in which the formed nanogold exhibits a strong RRS signal. In the presence of VBB, the system exhibits a strong Raman signal. The nanocatalytic reaction was studied by three molecular spectral techniques and the enhancement mechanism was discussed. Combined the CDN/Ag catalytic amplification and the immunoreaction, a SERS/RRS dual-spectroscopic quantitative analysis method with highly sensitivity and reproducibility is developed to detect Clen.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21667006, 21767004, 21465006, 21477025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2019.120529.
